segments.title: USS Independence exposes the Great Frigate tradeoff
segments.summary: America's first commissioned ship of the line becomes a case study in why impressive heavy frigates could not substitute for fleet battle power.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke introduces Independence as both the first US Navy ship of the line to serve and its only razee. He contrasts her fleet utility with the Great Frigates: excellent for presence and single-ship actions, but too costly for routine frigate numbers and unable to face British ships of the line.

evidence.note: The introduction identifies Independence's two distinctions and compares ships of the line with the Great Frigate procurement choice.

segments.title: National potential is not the same as deployable naval power
segments.summary: British assessments focused on the American government's fielded forces rather than the country's population, industry, or future potential.
segments.body: The lecture separates criticism of early US defense policy from judgments about the American people. In 1812, Britain could compare fewer than a dozen American frigates with hundreds of Royal Navy warships backed by decades of wartime finance and experience, producing an enormous gap in immediately usable power.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke distinguishes national potential from government capability and compares the two navies' scale.

segments.title: Impressment and neutral trade before the War of 1812
segments.summary: Conflicting citizenship rules, weak documentation, Caribbean trade, and blockade law made impressment a tangled dispute rather than a single clean grievance.
segments.body: British law treated British-born sailors as subjects even after American naturalization, while US documentation often could not establish a sailor's status. Neutral merchants also carried French colonial goods through American ports, drawing seizures from both sides of the Napoleonic conflict. The rejected Monroe-Pinkney settlement shows how close the governments came to resolving most issues without war.

evidence.note: The transcript explains naturalization, impressment records, neutral trade, seizures, and the proposed treaty settlement.

segments.title: Indigenous trade, Canada, and the war's competing motives
segments.summary: British arms-for-furs commerce, mistaken expectations of Canadian support, and expansionist calculations all fed the decision for war.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke treats weapons supplied to Indigenous communities mainly as merchant trade rather than a simple British plot, while recognizing its effect on American security. He also stresses that Quebecois inhabitants, Loyalist refugees, and newer British settlers were unlikely to welcome invasion. The resulting picture is a maelstrom of genuine disputes, domestic politics, and opportunism while Britain was occupied with Napoleon.

evidence.note: The lecture covers the arms trade, Canadian demographics, American procurement ideas, and rival interpretations of Madison's aims.

segments.title: Great Frigate victories meet the Royal Navy blockade
segments.summary: American frigates won celebrated actions, but British ships of the line progressively confined the US Navy and exposed its missing fleet tier.
segments.body: The heavier American frigates had decisive advantages over ordinary British frigates, yet that success ended when ships of the line arrived in strength. The blockade could concentrate on major ports and exploit regional opposition to the war rather than seal every mile of coastline. This strategic constraint led Americans to recognize that frigates alone were insufficient.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke contrasts frigate victories with blockade pressure and the realization that the navy lacked ships of the line.

segments.title: America repeatedly starts but rarely completes battle fleets
segments.summary: Continental, Columbus, Independence, Pennsylvania, Delaware, and later programs reveal a persistent gap between American ambition and completed ships.
segments.body: USS America went to France, several planned vessels disappeared, and only nine of twenty-eight started US ships of the line were completed for American service. Dr. Clarke argues that even a modest balanced force of ships of the line, heavy frigates, and ordinary cruisers would have better represented US wealth and intent. Pennsylvania's 130 guns demonstrate that technical capability was not the limiting factor.

evidence.note: The transcript surveys planned and completed American ships of the line and proposes a sustainable balanced fleet.

segments.title: The Independence class shows American shipbuilding quality
segments.summary: Franklin's gun decks, Washington's covered slip, and Columbus's sail plan display a sophisticated industrial base when funding was sustained.
segments.body: The sister ships look like powerful 74-gun third rates despite carrying heavier nominal batteries. Their construction arrangements and sail plans indicate careful workmanship and significant infrastructure. Dr. Clarke's criticism is therefore institutional: American projects were often completed to a very high standard or abandoned altogether.

evidence.note: The lecture uses the class's hulls, covered construction, and sail plans to assess American industrial quality.

segments.title: A powerful blockade keeps Independence in Boston
segments.summary: Several British 74s, the 98-gun Boyne, frigates, and smaller vessels made a sortie by the newly armed Independence strategically reckless.
segments.body: Independence launched and armed rapidly in 1814, but the Royal Navy knew she was coming and assembled overwhelming force off Boston. The example shows how Britain varied blockade strength according to the threat inside each port. Dr. Clarke connects that practice to wider Royal Navy experience monitoring European and Atlantic routes.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies the Boston blockade force and explains why Independence did not challenge it.

segments.title: HMS Nymphe's fireship raid nearly destroys Independence
segments.summary: British sailors and marines turned a captured sloop into a fireship and sent it toward the nearly completed American ship of the line.
segments.body: The attempt failed because defenders kept the burning vessel away from Independence, but it demonstrates detailed British knowledge of her location and readiness. Destroying the ship before commissioning would have reduced the force needed to blockade Boston and eliminated a future fleet threat at low cost.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke describes the captured sloop, fireship attack, defenders, and British preventive logic.

segments.title: Independence adapts the transatlantic 74-gun design tradition
segments.summary: Her hull and rig reflect an American interpretation of British adaptations to the original French 74, optimized for oceanic service.
segments.body: The French 74 supplied the starting point, while British practice widened and provisioned the type for long global deployments. Independence carried that design lineage into American construction with strong lines and a capable full rig. Dr. Clarke flags the story about using trees previously marked by Admiralty surveyors as attractive but unverified.

evidence.note: The lecture traces the design lineage and explicitly cautions that the marked-timber story is unverified.

segments.title: What did Independence's ninety 32-pounders actually mean?
segments.summary: A mixed battery of long guns, medium guns, and carronades better fits the ship's pierced ports and crew than ninety identical long guns.
segments.body: Although often listed as carrying ninety 32-pounders, that label does not specify gun type. Comparing sister Franklin's battery with Independence's 74 hull ports suggests long guns below, medium guns above, and carronades on the upper works. A crew of roughly 790 also appears too small to operate ninety long 32-pounders while sailing the ship.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke compares gun types, Franklin's documented mix, hull piercings, and manpower requirements.

segments.title: A Barbary show of force begins Independence's service
segments.summary: Independence led Bainbridge's 1815 Mediterranean squadron as a visible demonstration that the United States could now deploy battle-fleet power.
segments.body: Stephen Decatur had already compelled peace, so Independence's arrival reinforced the settlement rather than fighting another campaign. She returned to Newport and served as a flagship before congressional underfunding placed her in ordinary from 1822 to 1836. Her reserve years preserved an expensive asset for later conversion and service.

evidence.note: The transcript covers Bainbridge's Mediterranean deployment, return, flagship duty, and years in ordinary.

segments.title: Razeed into one of the US Navy's best frigates
segments.summary: Removing one deck in 1836 produced a fast, powerful 54-gun frigate but cost the navy one of its very few ships of the line.
segments.body: A razee gains the expensive hull quality of a ship of the line in frigate form, which explains Independence's excellent speed and sailing qualities. The tradeoff was strategic: even as Pennsylvania entered service, the United States remained near a ceiling of only seven or eight battle-line ships. The conversion favored a useful cruising flagship over scarce line-of-battle strength.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke explains the razee conversion, its performance benefits, and its effect on US battle-fleet numbers.

segments.title: Independence serves diplomacy, commerce, and the Mexican War
segments.summary: As a razee flagship, Independence carried a minister to Russia, protected South American commerce, blockaded Mexico, and landed forces at Mazatlan.
segments.body: Her 1837 voyage linked Portsmouth, Copenhagen, Kronstadt, and Rio before service with the Brazil Squadron. Commodore Nicholson's unauthorized attempt to mediate between France and Argentina also illustrates the limits of prestige without government backing. Recommissioned for war with Mexico, the ship supported blockade, captures, occupation ashore, and Pacific cruising before later Mediterranean duty.

evidence.note: The lecture follows the ship's Russian, Brazilian, Mexican War, Pacific, and Mediterranean assignments.

segments.title: A long receiving-ship career ends in destructive salvage
segments.summary: After Pacific flagship service, Independence spent more than fifty years at Mare Island before being burned in 1915 to recover metal fittings.
segments.body: The ship entered Mare Island in 1857 and remained a receiving ship until decommissioning in 1912. Plans to use her as a restaurant for the Panama-Pacific International Exposition failed, after which valuable timber and fittings were salvaged. Burning the first commissioned US ship of the line and the navy's only razee erased an exceptional physical link to the early fleet.

evidence.note: The transcript covers receiving-ship duty, failed exposition plans, salvage, and burning at Hunter's Point.

segments.title: Independence's larger lesson: reserves, hard power, and credibility
segments.summary: The ship's long usefulness supports investment in reserve battle fleets and shows how credible hard power changes diplomacy even when it does not prevent war.
segments.body: Years in ordinary did not make Independence wasteful; they preserved an expensive capability that could return for diplomacy, war, and distant stations. Dr. Clarke avoids claiming that prewar American ships of the line would certainly have prevented 1812, but argues they would have altered foreign assessments, alliance possibilities, and the Royal Navy commitment required. The conclusion connects fleet strength with confidence, deterrence, and the hard power needed to make legitimate demands credible.

evidence.note: The conclusion assesses reserve value, counterfactual fleet strength, alliance possibilities, deterrence, and diplomatic credibility.
